excerpt: According to Ismaili daʿwa doctrine, every nāṭiq (speaker-prophet) is prepared and authorised through the Imam who closes the previous cycle, so that the chain of authority from one cycle to the next is unbroken.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān ibn Muḥammad (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: On the relation between successive prophets and the Imams who confirm them

excerpt: The cosmos is graded as a hierarchy of ḥudūd (limits), and every level of the lower world subserves the manifestation of the higher; the procession of prophets is thus the temporal counterpart to that hierarchy, ordered toward the perfection of the Muḥammadan summit.
source_authority: Ḥamīd al-Dīn al-Kirmānī (d. ca. 411/1020), Ḥujjat al-ʿIrāqayn of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: On the hierarchy of ḥudūd and the finality of the seventh nāṭiq

excerpt: The disciplined soul that turns away from the appetites of this world finds in obedience to the sharīʿa not a burden but a delight; this transformation is the proper fruit of taʾyīd (divine support).
source_authority: al-Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Shīrāzī (d. 470/1078), chief dāʿī under al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: On the sweetness of obedience for the soul instructed by the daʿwa

excerpt: Be in the world as one who is passing through, and do not take it as a home; lower your gaze from it, and prepare for the journey.
source_authority: sayings of Imām ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, compiled by al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (d. 406/1015)
source_location_hint: From the counsels of Imām ʿAlī on detachment from the world

excerpt: True repentance requires that the servant return to God with the heart, restrain the tongue, and turn the limbs from the sin — for the sin of the heart is the gravest, since the heart is the seat of intention.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān ibn Muḥammad (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: Chapter on tawba and the obligations of the repentant

excerpt: The servant breaks before God in awareness of his fault, and through that very brokenness is drawn nearer to Him.
source_author: Imām Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn
source_location_hint: From the supplications on confession of sin and the seeking of forgiveness
